## Overview

In this exercise I learnt how to use `ggstatsplot` to create statistical graphics with rich statistical information embedded directly in the charts.

## Getting Started

### Loading packages


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
pacman::p_load(ggstatsplot, tidyverse)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

The downloaded binary packages are in
	/var/folders/dj/gj3ytvh900z09pcdgsdwpm900000gn/T//RtmpKceI9Y/downloaded_packages
```


:::
:::


### Importing data


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
exam <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")
```
:::


## One-sample test: gghistostats()

Testing whether the mean English score is significantly different from 60.

::: panel-tabset

## The plot


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hands-on_Ex04b_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## The code chunk


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
set.seed(1234)

gghistostats(
  data = exam,
  x = ENGLISH,
  type = "bayes",
  test.value = 60,
  xlab = "English scores"
)
```
:::


:::

## Two-sample mean test: ggbetweenstats()

Comparing Maths scores between genders using a non-parametric test.

::: panel-tabset

## The plot


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hands-on_Ex04b_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-5-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## The code chunk


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggbetweenstats(
  data = exam,
  x = GENDER,
  y = MATHS,
  type = "np",
  messages = FALSE
)
```
:::


:::

## Oneway ANOVA Test: ggbetweenstats()

Comparing English scores across different races with pairwise comparisons.

::: panel-tabset

## The plot


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hands-on_Ex04b_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-7-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## The code chunk


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggbetweenstats(
  data = exam,
  x = RACE,
  y = ENGLISH,
  type = "p",
  mean.ci = TRUE,
  pairwise.comparisons = TRUE,
  pairwise.display = "s",
  p.adjust.method = "fdr",
  messages = FALSE
)
```
:::


:::

## Significant Test of Correlation: ggscatterstats()

Testing whether there is a significant correlation between Maths and English scores.

::: panel-tabset

## The plot


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hands-on_Ex04b_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-9-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## The code chunk


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggscatterstats(
  data = exam,
  x = MATHS,
  y = ENGLISH,
  marginal = FALSE
)
```
:::


:::

## Significant Test of Association: ggbarstats()

Testing the association between Maths results (pass/fail) and gender.

::: panel-tabset

## The plot


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Hands-on_Ex04b_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-11-1.png){width=672}
:::
:::


## The code chunk


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
exam1 <- exam %>%
  mutate(MATHS_bins =
           cut(MATHS,
               breaks = c(0,60,75,85,100))
)

ggbarstats(exam1,
           x = MATHS_bins,
           y = GENDER)
```
:::


:::

## Reflections

`ggstatsplot` is really useful because it combines the chart and the statistical test result in one — something that would normally take two separate steps in R. The `ggbetweenstats()` function was the most interesting since it automatically runs the right test depending on the number of groups and shows the pairwise comparisons directly on the chart.

The Bayes Factor in `gghistostats()` was new to me — it gives a more intuitive way to interpret evidence compared to just a p-value.

## References
- [ggstatsplot documentation](https://indrajeetpatil.github.io/ggstatsplot/)
- [R for Visual Analytics Chapter 10](https://r4va.netlify.app/chap10)
